data.title: Wall Street rises as traders assess the latest labor report; eyes on Middle East tensions
data.description:
The stock market rose on Wednesday as traders continued to monitor developments in the Middle East and digested the better-than-expected labor report.

The S&P 500 (SP500) +0.3%, the Dow (DJI) +0.1%, and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) +0.8%.

U.S. private sector employment grew by 63K jobs in February, topping the +43K consensus and accelerating from the 11K added in January, according to data released by ADP on Wednesday.

Oil inched lower, with crude oil futures (CL1:COM) down 0.09% to $74.49.

U.S. Treasury yields moved higher across the curve. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 1.6 basis points to 4.09%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) advanced 3.5 basis points to 3.54%. The 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) moved up 2.4 basis points to 4.73%.

data.title: Most and least shorted mid-to mega-cap healthcare stocks in February
data.description:
The healthcare sector is facing aggressive short selling, particularly targeting high-growth disruptors while avoiding major pharmaceutical companies. As of late February, short interest in volatile areas like telehealth and biotech has reached record highs.

Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) leads the bearish list with a 36.90% short interest, the telehealth leader is facing intense skepticism regarding its long-term margins and the sustainability of its compounded GLP-1 offerings. At the same time, Acadia Healthcare (ACHC) is the second-most shorted stock in the sector at 35.19%.

In contrast, some healthcare giants like Eli Lilly (LLY) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) have very low short interest at 0.71% and 0.86%, respectively. They are joined by industry leaders Bausch + Lomb Corporation (BLCO), Medline (MDLN), and Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO), all of which maintain short interest levels well below 1%, because they are considered much safer bets during uncertain times.

Healthcare stocks are outperforming the broader market so far this year. While the S&P 500 has struggled with a 0.42% decline year-to-date, the Health Care Select Sector SPDR® Fund (XLV)—which represents 12.12% of the benchmark index—has climbed 1.25%.

Here are the top 5 most shorted mid- to mega-cap healthcare stocks (as a % of shares outstanding) as of February 28:

Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) - Health Care Services sector, short interest 36.90%

Acadia Healthcare Company (ACHC) - Health Care Facilities sector, short interest 35.19%

TransMedics Group (TMDX) - Health Care Equipment sector, short interest 23.98%

CRISPR Therapeutics (CRSP) – Biotechnology sector, short interest 23.59%

Viking Therapeutics (VKTX) – Biotechnology sector, short interest 22.64%

Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) – Pharmaceuticals sector, short interest 0.71%

Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) – Pharmaceuticals sector, short interest 0.86%

Bausch + Lomb Corporation (BLCO) - Health Care Supplies sector, short interest 0.89%

Medline (MDLN) - Health Care Supplies sector, short interest 0.90%

Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) - Life Sciences Tools and Services sector, short interest 0.96%

data.title: Trump's global 15% tariff rate to start this week, Bessent says: CNBC
data.description:
The Trump administration will likely implement the 15% global tariff rate sometime this week, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a CNBC interview. 

In February, President Donald Trump had said that he would increase his newly announced global tariff level to 15% from 10% after the U.S. Supreme Court struck down most of the tariffs he imposed last year.

In the CNBC interview, Bessent also said that the tariff rate would return to where it was before the Supreme Court struck it down by August. 

“It’s my strong belief that the tariff rates will be back to their old rate within five months,” Bessent said in an interview on CNBC’s “Squawk Box.”

The Supreme Court issued a landmark judgment, invalidating most of Trump's previously imposed trade levies in a 6-3 ruling. 

“They have survived more than 4,000 legal challenges,” he said. “They are more slow-moving, but they are more robust,” Bessent said in the interview. 

Separately, the European Union does not expect the U.S. to up the universal tariff rate to 15% on the bloc, according to the Bloomberg report. 

The EU has reportedly received assurances that the U.S. will keep a 10% tariff rate for the bloc's exports, Bloomberg said, citing people familiar with the matter. 

Dear readers, we recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: EU proposes industrial accelerator law to bolster clean-tech manufacturing
data.description:
The European Union has proposed new measures aimed at strengthening domestic production of low-carbon technologies as European manufacturers face mounting competition from lower-cost Chinese rivals, Dow Jones Newswires reported.

Stephane Sejourne, the European Commission’s industry chief, introduced the proposed Industrial Accelerator Act on Wednesday. The legislation is designed to support companies producing clean technologies such as wind turbines and electric-vehicle batteries within Europe.

Faster approvals and local-content goals

The proposal would seek to speed up permitting tied to public procurement projects while also introducing minimum requirements for certain components to be manufactured within the European Union. The goal is to encourage more investment in local manufacturing capacity for emerging green technologies.

“What I am presenting to you [Wednesday] is more than just a change in operating procedures, it is a change in doctrine—something that was unthinkable a few months ago,” Sejourne told reporters in Brussels.

New standards and investment safeguards

Under the proposal, the EU would also create a “green” label for steel to help buyers assess the carbon footprint of the products they purchase. The legislation would require low-carbon standards and EU-origin content in certain government procurement processes.

The plan also aims to tighten scrutiny of foreign investment in sectors where global production is heavily concentrated. Those industries include electric vehicles, batteries, solar technology and critical raw materials.

data.title: Elektros says U.S. grants patent for multi-port EV charging system
data.description:
• Elektros (ELEK) on Wednesday said that the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office granted it U.S. Patent No. 12,522,100 B1 covering a multi-port charging assembly for electric vehicles.
  • The company said the patented system is designed to combine multiple independent power inputs through a single charging interface, aiming to reduce electric vehicle charging times while remaining compatible with existing vehicle standards.

  • Elektros said the patent, issued on January 13, 2026, includes 20 claims and carries a 713-day patent term extension under U.S. law.

  • The invention was developed by Chief Executive Shlomo Bleier and is assigned to the company.

  • The company said the technology could be applied across passenger, commercial and specialty electric vehicle platforms.
  • ELEK closed +45.0% at $0.0087.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Moderna gains as Arbutus/ Genevant settlement removes major financial risk
data.description:
Moderna (MRNA) shares continued to rise in the premarket on Wednesday as analysts argued that the company’s agreement to resolve a patent dispute with Arbutus Biopharma (ABUS) and the Roivant unit (ROIV), Genevant Sciences, removed a key legal overhang for the COVID vaccine maker.

Shares of Moderna (MRNA) rose in the post-market on Tuesday after the Cambridge, Massachusetts-based biotech agreed to pay $950M to settle a legal dispute related to the delivery technology behind its COVID-19 vaccine.

Under the agreement, Moderna (MRNA) will also appeal to the Federal Circuit to argue that it has government-contractor immunity, which limits its liability. If the court rules against the argument, Moderna (MRNA) is expected to pay up to an additional $1.3B within 90 days of that decision.

According to Bloomberg, Citi analyst Geoff Meacham, with a Neutral rating on MRNA, argued that the deal does “remove an overhang for the stock,” while it is “not the best-case scenario but better than many had feared.”

William Blair analyst Myles Minter saw “Moderna moving past this litigation overhang as a positive clearing event.” Minter, with a Market Perform rating on Moderna (MRNA), added that “the settlement value was better than feared.”

J.P. Morgan’s Brian Cheng opined that while the company has to pay up to $2.3B under the agreement, it was lower than the $5B estimated for the litigation. “Taking the case through a jury trial could, in our view, add another layer of uncertainty," Cheng wrote with an Overweight rating on Roivant (ROIV).

Cantor Fitzgerald’s Prakhar Agrawal, with a similar rating on ROIV, forecasts a very high probability for Moderna (MRNA) paying $1.3B additionally, as Section 1498, the federal statute that the company plans to appeal, “has already been decided in Genevant’s/Arbutus' favor multiple times."

data.title: Apple takes wraps off new low-cost MacBook Neo
data.description:
• Marking its third day of product announcements, Apple (AAPL) unveiled its new low-cost MacBook Neo on Wednesday.
  • The new MacBook is powered by the A18 Pro processor, which first appeared in the iPhone 16 Pro, Apple said. The MacBook Neo will start at $599 and is aimed at competing with low-cost Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) Chromebooks and the Microsoft (MSFT) Surface.
  • On Monday, Apple unveiled the iPhone 17e and an updated version of the iPad Air. That was followed up with new versions of the MacBook Air, MacBook Pro, and new Studio Displays.
  • Following the release of the MacBook Neo, the tech giant will host an “Apple Experience” in New York, London, and Shanghai this morning to show off the newly announced  electronics.

This is breaking news...

data.title: Elutia regains full Nasdaq listing compliance
data.description:
• Elutia (ELUT) has regained compliance with Nasdaq listing standards.
  • The company met the minimum bid price requirement.
  • Elutia also satisfied the minimum market value of listed securities requirement.
  • ELUT shares up 6.5% premarket.
